findings.description: User-controlled name_pattern is compiled and executed as an unbounded regex (re.compile/search) with no timeout, length cap, or complexity limit, enabling ReDoS via catastrophic backtracking (e.g. '(a+)+$'), amplified by running over every non-deleted user row.

An invalid name_pattern causes re.compile to raise re.error which propagates unhandled to the caller, so a malformed (attacker-trivial) query becomes an unhandled exception rather than rejected input.

The entire non-deleted users table is materialized into memory via fetchall() and a list comprehension before pagination is applied, making large tables a memory/CPU resource-exhaustion (DoS) vector independent of page_size.

The page parameter is not bounds-checked, so a negative page yields a negative offset and Python negative-index slicing returns an unintended window from the end of the result set (primarily a logic bug; defer detail to the logic critic).

Scanner-flagged Log Injection appears to be a false positive: the log call interpolates only integers via %d (len(window), total, page) and no untrusted string reaches the log record, so CRLF/log-forging is not achievable here.

No authorization is enforced in the module: deactivate() lets any caller soft-delete an arbitrary user by id and by_email enables enumeration, which is only safe if every caller enforces access control (low confidence given these are documented public entry points).
